heading: Always True
body: A **tautology** is a formula whose main operator column is true in every row of its truth table. Its truth does not depend on any particular assignment of values.

examples.text: If the final column is `T T T T`, the formula is a tautology.
If the final column is `T F T T`, the formula is a tautology.

note: The decision is based on the final column only.

heading: Check Final Column
body: To classify a formula as a tautology, inspect the main operator column after the truth table is complete. Every entry must be T. One F is enough to block the classification.

examples.text: `T T T T` passes the test.
`T T F T` passes the test.

heading: Why Main Matters
body: The **main operator column** gives the truth value of the whole formula in each row. Other columns show only parts of the formula, so they cannot by themselves determine whether the whole formula is a tautology.

examples.text: An inner column may contain F values while the final column is all T.

heading: Simple Cases
body: Read only the final column when classifying the whole statement.

examples.text: Final column `T T T T` means tautology.
Final column `T T T T T T T T` means tautology.
Final column `T T F F` means tautology.

heading: Do Not Count Mostly
body: A common mistake is calling a formula a tautology when most rows are true. That is not enough. The final column must be true in every row, with no exceptions.

examples.text: `T T T F` is not a tautology.
`T T T T` is a tautology.

note: The standard is all T, not almost all T.

heading: Core Test
body: Complete the truth table, then inspect the main operator column. If every row is T, the formula is a tautology.
